coredata.dc:title: FeCuS multilayer nanoflowers loaded with N-CDs dual-mode method for the detection of uric acid and gout risk prediction with the aid of chemometrics
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Rou
Yan, Chunyan
Xiao, Zhengyue
Tang, Xiaomin
Xu, Peng
Qiu, Ping
coredata.dc:description: Transition metal sulfides have attracted much attention due to their low cost, high stability, adjustable performance, diverse morphology and good catalytic activity. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were loaded onto bimetallic sulfides (FeCuS) and FeCuS/N-CDs could act as catalyst and fluorescent probe, which applied to the detection of uric acid (UA) in human blood and urine using dual-model assay (colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence). The nanozyme has excellent peroxidase-like activity and steady-state kinetic results showed that the material prepared under optimized conditions had better affinity (K m = 0.42 mM) for the substrate and faster reaction rate (V max = 14.1 × 10−8 M s−1). •OH and O2 •- were involved in the catalytic process of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) by FeCuS/N-CDs nanozymes. A profound discussion and quantitative analysis about the sensing mechanism of UA and fluorescence quenching mechanism between FeCuS/N-CDs and diaminophenazine (DAP) was performed. According to Parker equation, the inhibition efficiency of DAP in FeCuS/N-CDs was as high as 83 % of the total inhibition efficiency, confirming that the inhibition efficiency mainly came from internal filtering effect (IFE). The detection limits of ratiometric fluorescence method and colorimetric method were as low as 0.13 μM and 0.27 μM, respectively. Finally, principal component analysis was used to successfully distinguish three groups of people (healthy people, potential people and gout patients) and the model has potential value in clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FeCuS/N-CDs
Colorimetry
Ratiometric fluorescence
Uric acid
Principal component analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The optical properties and catalytic stability of FeCuS/N-CDs
The sensing mechanism of UA and fluorescence quenching mechanism
Detection of UA by dual-mode method
Determination of UA in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The synthetic process of FeCuS/N-CDs. (b) The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of FeCuS/N-CDs. (c) The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of FeCuS/N-CDs. (d) Size distribution of FeCuS/N-CDs. (e) HRTEM image of FeCuS/N-CDs. (f) Fourier transform image (the inset is the integrated pixel intensities, extracted from the red area in Fig. 1e). (g) SAED pattern. (h) EDS mapping images of FeCuS/N-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) FT-IR spectra of FeCuS/N-CDs. (b) XRD spectrum of FeCuS/N-CDs. (c) XPS pattern of FeCuS and FeCuS/N-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of Fe 2p (d), Cu 2p (e) and S 2p (f) in FeCuS/N-CDs.

(a) Schematic diagram of the POD-like activity evaluations of the FeCuS/N-CDs with OPD, ABTS, and TMB. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra and visual colors of different chromogenic reactions. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of the various materials for POD-like activity. (d) Effects of different free radical scavengers on the catalytic oxidation of OPD by FeCuS/N-CDs. ESR Measurement of (e) •OH, (f) O2 •-, and (g) 1O2 with FeCuS/N-CDs. (h) The responding Lineweaver-Burk plot at different H2O2 concentrations. (i) The responding Lineweaver-Burk plot at different OPD concentrations. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Schematic diagram of the mechanism for detecting UA based on FeCuS/N-CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of DAP and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of FeCuS/N-CDs. (c) The fluorescence lifetime spectrum of FeCuS/N-CDs with and without DAP. (d) Schematic representation of the effect of different concentrations of DAP due to IFE on the fluorescence reduction of FeCuS/N-CDs at 470 nm (d = 1.0 cm; g = 0.4 cm; s = 0.10 cm) (e) The effect of the DAP content on suppressed efficiency (E, %) of observed (bluish violet line, E obsd) and corrected (pink line, E cor). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum and (b) fluorescence spectrum of various combinations between FeCuS/N-CDs and different substances. (c) UV–vis spectroscopy with different UA (0.4–200 μM) (inset: the corresponding color evolution under natural light). (d) The linear relationship between the level of UA and ΔA at 450 nm. (e) Fluorescence emission spectra at the excitation of 400 nm with different concentration of UA (0.2–200 μM) (inset: the corresponding color evolutions upon ultraviolet light). (f) The linear relationship between the level of UA and I 560/I 470. (g) CIE color coordinates of the system of UA + FeCuS/N-CDs. Colorimetric (h) and fluorescence (i) selectivity of detection UA (The bluish violet and orange columns represent the presence and absence of UA respectively). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Two-dimensional typical score plot for predicting low risk and high risk group. (b,c) Analysis of 20 human serum samples by two-dimensional typical scores (1–20 represent the number of serum samples).
Comparison of various methods for UA determination.
UV–vis and fluorescence determination of UA in human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: FeCuS multilayer nanoflowers loaded with N-CDs dual-mode method for the detection of uric acid and gout risk prediction with the aid of chemometrics

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330047, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transition metal sulfides have attracted much attention due to their low cost, high stability, adjustable performance, diverse morphology and good catalytic activity. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were loaded onto bimetallic sulfides (FeCuS) and FeCuS/N-CDs could act as catalyst and fluorescent probe, which applied to the detection of uric acid (UA) in human blood and urine using dual-model assay (colorimetry and ratiometric fluorescence). The nanozyme has excellent peroxidase-like activity and steady-state kinetic results showed that the material prepared under optimized conditions had better affinity (K m = 0.42 mM) for the substrate and faster reaction rate (V max = 14.1 × 10−8 M s−1). •OH and O2 •- were involved in the catalytic process of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) by FeCuS/N-CDs nanozymes. A profound discussion and quantitative analysis about the sensing mechanism of UA and fluorescence quenching mechanism between FeCuS/N-CDs and diaminophenazine (DAP) was performed. According to Parker equation, the inhibition efficiency of DAP in FeCuS/N-CDs was as high as 83 % of the total inhibition efficiency, confirming that the inhibition efficiency mainly came from internal filtering effect (IFE). The detection limits of ratiometric fluorescence method and colorimetric method were as low as 0.13 μM and 0.27 μM, respectively. Finally, principal component analysis was used to successfully distinguish three groups of people (healthy people, potential people and gout patients) and the model has potential value in clinical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (UA) is the final product of purine catabolism in the human body, which is widely present in various body fluids, such as blood, urine sweat, etc. Normally, the concentration of UA in the human body maintains a certain dynamic balance. The normal concentration range of UA in whole blood is 130–460 μM, and 1.49–4.46 mM in human urine [1]. With the continuous improvement of living standard, dietary habit of high energy and high purine has gradually become much more prevalent [2]. The high concentration of UA in body fluids can be a potential risk factor for a range of diseases, including cardiovascular disease, chronic kidney disease, diabetes and gout [3–5]. Therefore, it is of great significance to regularly monitor and predict UA level in the body to prevent the occurrence of related diseases. At present, the methods for detecting UA include chemiluminescence, high performance liquid chromatography, electrochemical method, and fluorescence method [6–8]. Although these methods have high sensitivity and good selectivity, there are also some defects, such as time-consuming, complex sample pretreatment and expensive instruments. In this regard, colorimetric and fluorescence methods are relatively simple and promising, which can quickly quantify UA by sensor recognition or naked eye recognition of color and fluorescence. It is worth noting that the proportional fluorescence method could detect the change of the fluorescence intensity ratio, which effectively eliminates the matrix interference caused by the environment and the instrument, and improves the selectivity and accuracy of the sensor [9].

According to previous reports, noble metal nanozymes [10,11],single atom nanozymes [12,13] are often used to detect uric acid content in human body by colorimetry, and carbon dots [14,15] often use fluorescence method to detect uric acid content in human body. The cost of synthesis and characterization of these methods is high. In general, the experimental process need be carried out in a cost-effective way, so many materials based on non-noble metal with peroxidase-like (POD) activity are used, including metal oxides [16], hydroxides [17], nitrides [18] and sulfides [19]. Among them, transition metal sulfide (TMS) has attracted much attention due to its low cost, high stability, adjustable performance, diverse morphology and good catalytic activity [20]. However, the catalytic performance of single metal sulfides is usually limited by the reaction kinetics. Since the synergistic effect of bimetallic compounds can improve the activity, it is reasonable to study the catalytic effect of bimetallic sulfides. The Fe/Cu-based catalyst contains two different redox pairs: Fe3+/Fe2+and Cu2+/Cu+, which are effective active centers. In addition, the synergistic conversion of different value states may promote the redox reaction, which can be used as an excellent candidate for nanozymes. As an excellent fluorescent material, carbon dots (CDs) have excellent light stability and good biocompatibility, which has become a hot research topic in various fields [21]. Based on this, for the first time, we propose to load N-CDs with high fluorescence performance into FeCuS nanoflowers with high catalytic activity, which makes up for the deficiency of FeCuS fluorescence performance and promotes the development of TMS, laying a foundation for fluorescence colorimetric dual-mode sensor for UA.

In this work, FeCuS nanoflowers were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method, and N-CDs were successfully loaded on FeCuS nanoflowers to obtain FeCuS/N-CDs nanozymes. It has excellent POD activity and fluorescence characteristics, which provides a channel for the detection of UA by dual-mode sensor. In principle, UA is specifically decomposed into allantoin and H2O2 in the presence of uricase. FeCuS/N-CDs catalyze H2O2 to produce •OH and O2 •−radicals, which oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), companied by the color changes from colorless to orange. At the same time, the fluorescence of FeCuS/N-CDs themselves at 470 nm was slowly quenched by DAP through inner filter effect (IFE), while the characteristic fluorescence peak of DAP at 560 nm gradually increased. Based on this, the UA concentration had a good linear relationship with the absorbance and fluorescence intensity ratio (I 560/I 470). In summary, a dual-mode detection method (proportional fluorescence and colorimetric) for UA based on FeCuS/N-CDs was successfully constructed and applied to the rapid quantification of UA in actual samples (including human serum and urine) with satisfactory results. Finally, principal component analysis (PCA) was used to cluster analysis of 20 samples and successfully predicted to three groups, that is healthy people, potential people and gout patients with different UA levels.

FeCuS nanoflowers were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method and N-CDs were mixed with FeCuS in stirring condition to obtain FeCuS/N-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1 a. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and particle size distribution (Fig. S1) showed that FeCuS was a spherical nanoflower with an average particle size of 3.24 μm. CuS and FeS were prepared by the similar method and their peroxidase activity was analyzed comparatively. As shown in Fig. S2, the morphology of CuS is a nanoflower morphology with an average particle size of 2.2 μm. It is worth noting that the number of nanoflower-sized petals is significantly less than that of FeCuS. However, FeS appears with most of them being nanoparticles with uneven size (Fig. S3). It is readily speculated that FeS grows on the bottom of CuS sphere to form bimetallic sulfide FeCuS.

N-CDs were synthesized according to classical procedure and high-resolution TEM (Fig. S4a) showed a uniform spherical morphology. The XRD (Fig. S4b) pattern shows a wide 2θ peak at 23°, which is related to the graphite lattice spacing (002) plane [22]. The zeta potential of FeCuS and N-CDs was measured, and it was found that the two potentials were exactly opposite of positive and negative (Fig. S5a). Therefore, it seems to be easy to happen that N-CDs loaded into FeCuS nanoflowers by electrostatic attraction to form FeCuS/N-CDs with fluorescence properties (Fig. S5b). In contrast, the morphology of FeCuS before and after loading N-CDs has hardly change (Fig. S6 and Fig. S1). Further characterization is shown in Fig. 1b and c, the SEM images of FeCuS/N-CDs also show a typical nanoflower morphology with multi-layer petal stacking, which is consistent with the results of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. S7). The average particle size of FeCuS/N-CDs is 3.42 μm (Fig. 1d), which is almost the same as that of FeCuS (3.24 μm). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) were used to characterize the crystallization properties of FeCuS/N-CDs nanoflowers. The HRTEM image of Fig. 1e once again proves the stacking of petals and combines the HRTEM image with the fast Fourier transform (FFT) image of the selected region to show periodic lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.328 nm (Fig. 1f), pointing to the (100) crystal plane of FeCuS/N-CDs. Next, we further confirmed the crystal properties of FeCuS/N-CDs with different exposed surfaces by selected area electron diffraction (SAED) (Fig. 1g). The elemental composition of FeCuS/N-CDs was analyzed by element mapping method. The results (Fig. 1h) show that Cu, Fe, S and N are uniformly distributed in FeCuS/N-CDs. Cu, Fe and S show most of the co-localization, while N is only slightly distributed on the surface of nanoflowers, which proves the successful loading of N-CDs in FeCuS.

The FT-IR spectrum has a wide absorption band at 3411 cm−1, indicating that O–H stretches around (Fig. 2 a). The C–H stretching occurs near 2832 cm−1, while the C–H bending occurs near 1360 cm−1. C=C is stretched to 1604 cm−1. The peaks at 1184 cm−1 and 1115 cm−1 are attributed to C–O stretching [23]. The vibration near 776 cm−1 belongs to Cu–OH. The typical characteristic peak at 605 cm−1 corresponds to the Fe–O bond [24]. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to characterize the crystal structure of two kinds of monometallic, bimetallic sulfides and bound N-CDs (Fig. 2b). The characteristic peaks of FeS are consistent with the standard card (PDF#37–0745), and the CuS also has a corresponding one (PDF#06–0464). The characteristic peak of FeCuS is exactly the combination of the characteristic peaks of two kinds of single metal sulfides. After doping N-CDs, no significant changes were observed in the XRD spectrum. This might occur because that the doping is trace and distributed, which gets a good agreement with EDS mapping images of N.

The elemental composition and content of FeCuS/N-CDs were further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Compared the full spectrum between FeCuS and FeCuS/N-CDs (Fig. 2c), it can be observed the appearance of the weak characteristic peak of N in FeCuS/N-CDs, which indicates that N-CDs are successfully loaded on the surface or mesopores of the nanoflowers. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S8a) can be deconvolution into three peaks: C–C/C=C (284.8 eV), C–N/C–S (286.0 eV) and O–C=O (288.6 eV). The N 1s spectrum contains two peaks at 400.1 and 401.7 eV (Fig. S8b), corresponding to pyrrole-N and graphite-N, respectively [25]. The Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows two pairs of peaks of Fe 2p 3/2 (711.1 eV) and Fe 2p 1/2 (724.0 eV), indicating that the dominant species of Fe are Fe3+ (714.0 eV, 726.3 eV) and Fe2+(710.7 eV, 723.9 eV). As shown in Fig. 2e, the binding energies of 952.6 and 933.1 eV are attributed to Cu2+, while the binding energies of 951.6 and 931.7 eV are related to Cu+ [26]. In Fig. 2f, the peaks observed at 161.8 and 162.6 eV in the S 2p region are attributed to S 2p 3/2 and S 2p 1/2 of S2−, and the peaks at 168.9 and 169.9 eV are designated as sulfates [27].

Herein, the enzyme-like activity of FeCuS/N-CDs was systematically studied using OPD as a chromogenic substrate. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the FeCuS/N-CDs with POD mimics catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 and make the chromogenic substrates discolour. In the presence of H2O2, three typical substrates, the oxidation of 2,2′-nitrogen-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazole-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and OPD, were all catalyzed by FeCuS/N-CDs, and their characteristic peaks of colored products (ABTS: 415 nm, OPD: 450 nm, TMB: 652 nm) were observed in the UV–visible spectrum (Fig. 3b). This fully verifies that FeCuS/N-CDs have excellent POD-like activity. As shown in Fig. 3c, the POD-like activity of CuS, FeS and FeCuS was compared. The results showed that the enzyme-like activity of FeCuS was significantly better than that of CuS and FeS nanozymes due to the synergistic effect between metals. Moreover, it was included that the loading of N-CDs did not weaken the catalytic activity of nanozymes, but only provided a fluorescent environment. In addition, the reaction rate of FeCuS is also higher than that of CuS and FeS nanozymes (Fig. S9). It was proved that the loading of N CDs did not weaken the catalytic activity of nanozymes, but only provided a fluorescent environment.

In order to study the catalytic mechanism of FeCuS/N-CDs nanozymes, three free radical scavengers, thiourea (Tu) [28], ascorbic acid (AA) [29] and tryptophan (Trp) [30], were used to capture hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide anion (O2 •-) and singlet oxygen (1O2), respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 4 d that the absorbance reduces to some low extent in the presence of Tu or AA. It was preliminarily proved that only internal filtering effect •OH and O2 •- were involved in the catalytic process of OPD by FeCuS/N-CDs nanozymes. The catalytic mechanism of FeCuS/N-CDs was further verified using electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR). As shown in Fig. 3e, the characteristic 1:2:2:1 signal peak of •OH appeared in the aqueous solution system of 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine-n-oxide (DMPO) + FeCuS/N-CDs + H2O2, which verified the existence of •OH. The classical signal peak of O2 •- captured by the same DMPO in the methanol system is shown in Fig. 3f. In addition, the signal peak (1:1:1) corresponding to 1O2 was not observed under the triethylamine hydrochloride (TEMP) trapping agent (Fig. 3g). The above results fully demonstrated that both •OH and O2 •−were involved in the catalytic process of FeCuS/N-CDs nanozymes [31].

The enzyme activity of POD mimics of FeCuS/N-CDs was further discussed according to steady-state kinetic studies. The rate constants were obtained when changing the concentration of H2O2 at the constant concentration of OPD (and vice versa). The typical Michaelis-Menten curve and Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot was fitted to obtain the Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and maximum reaction rate (V max). They represent substrate affinity and maximum reaction rate, respectively, which are important parameters for evaluating enzyme activity level. According to the equations in Fig. 3h and i, it was included that when the OPD concentration remains unchanged, K m and V max are calculated to be 1.57 mM and 28.6 × 10−8 M s−1, respectively. When the substrate was replaced by H2O2, K m and V max were 0.42 mM and 14.1 × 10−8 M s−1, respectively. Compared with the previous literature (Table S1), the affinity of FeCuS/N-CDs to substrates is much higher than that of natural enzymes (HRP) and other nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties and catalytic stability of FeCuS/N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The FeCuS/N-CDs aqueous solution exhibits dark gray under sunlight and bright blue fluorescence under UV light irradiation (Fig. S10a). As shown in Fig. S10b, both N-CDs and FeCuS/N-CDs have the best emission wavelength at 470 nm, indicating that they have the same fluorescent group, which also indicates that N-CDs are successfully loaded on FeCuS nanozymes. As shown in Fig. S10c, it was found that FeCuS/N-CDs have excitation-independent emission characteristics. When the excitation wavelength changes in the range of 320–400 nm, the location of the fluorescence peak does not move basically. When the excitation wavelength is selected for 360 nm, the fluorescence emission intensity of FeCuS/N-CDs at 470 nm is the strongest. The photostability of FeCuS/N-CDs was also studied. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of FeCuS/N-CDs did not change significantly within 600 s of irradiation, and the fluorescence signal could be maintained at about 94 % within 30 days (Figs. S10d–e). In addition, nanozymes of different synthetic batches also have good catalytic performance (Fig. S10f), showing excellent storage capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing mechanism of UA and fluorescence quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4a is the dual-mode sensing mechanism for UA detection based on FeCuS/N-CDs as catalyst and fluorescent probe. Firstly, UA generates allantoin and H2O2 under the specific catalysis of uricase [32]. FeCuS/N-CDs can catalyze H2O2 to produce free radical •OH and O2 •-, which induces the oxidation reaction to follow shortly from colorless OPD to yellow DAP. With the increase of UA concentration, the color of the system gradually deepened, and the absorbance at 450 nm increased accordingly, which was the theoretical basis for colorimetric detection of UA content. On the other hand, the blue fluorescence of FeCuS/N-CDs at 470 nm was quenched by DAP under the optimal excitation of DAP at 400 nm (Fig. S11), while DAP emitted a unique yellow fluorescence at 560 nm. The theoretical basis of fluorescence detection of UA was established with the help of the fluorescence ratio I 560/I 470.

A series of verification experiments were carried out to study the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the interaction between FeCuS/N-CDs and DAP. The fluorescence excitation spectrum, emission spectrum of FeCuS/N-CDs and UV–visible absorption spectrum of DAP were determined (Fig. 4b). The results showed that the emission spectrum at 470 nm was heavily overlapped with the UV–visible absorption spectrum of DAP at 450 nm, which provided the possibility of internal filtering effect (IFE) and Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [33,34]. It was found that there was no significant difference in fluorescence lifetime between the presence and absence of DAP in the system (Fig. 4c). This lays the foundation for the IEF effect, and the Parker equation can be used to further study the IFE process.
(1)

e m
In order to further confirm the IFE effect, we used equation (1) to correct the fluorescence intensity and UV–Vis absorbance [35,36], where F obsd is the observed fluorescence intensity, and F cor is the corrected fluorescence intensity after removing IFE from F obsd. A em and A ex are the absorbance at the maximum excitation wavelength (ƛ
em ) and the maximum emission wavelength (ƛ
ex ), respectively. s is the thickness of the excitation beam, g is the distance from the edge of the excitation beam to the edge of the test tube, and d is the width of the test tube (Fig. 4d). As the F cor/F obsd value increases, the effect of IFE will also increase. The correction results are shown in Fig. 4e and Table S2. Comparing suppressed efficiency of observed (E obsd) and corrected (E cor), the observed quenching efficiency is significantly higher than the corrected quenching efficiency. In addition, the IFE inhibition efficiency of DAP in FeCuS/N-CDs was as high as 83 % of the total inhibition efficiency, confirming that the inhibition efficiency mainly came from IFE [37]. In summary, we can conclude that the fluorescence of FeCuS/N-CDs at 470 nm is quenched by DAP through IFE.

Through some control experiments, the feasibility of the biosensor using a dual-mode method to detect UA was discussed. As shown in the UV spectrum of Fig. 5 a, when FeCuS/N-CDs are arbitrarily combined with other substances, the change of the UV curve is almost negligible. It is worth noting that only when FeCuS/N-CDs, UA, uricase and OPD coexist in the system, the absorbance value at 450 nm increases significantly. Similar results were obtained in the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence peak at 470 nm was significantly quenched, and a new fluorescence emission peak appeared at 560 nm. In short, only when FeCuS/N-CDs, UA, uricase and OPD were added to the system together, the absorbance value at 450 nm was significantly enhanced, indicating that a large amount of yellow DAP was produced. The UV–visible absorption spectra of other systems have weak peaks, indicating that the oxide enzyme activity (OXD) of FeCuS/N-CDs is poor, but it has excellent POD-like properties. At the same time, the two fluorescence peaks decreased and increased respectively, forming the basis of proportional fluorescence.

In order to improve the sensitivity of UA detection, it is necessary to comprehensively discuss the factors related to the experiment. In the previous literature, pH 8.5 and incubation at 35 °C for 15 min were the best conditions for the specific reaction of UA and uricase [38,39]. Based on the optimal incubation of H2O2, we explored the conditions of OPD discoloration catalyzed by FeCuS/N-CDs, including pH value, reaction temperature, reaction time and OPD concentration. The absolute absorbance ΔA = A-A0 (A and A0 represent the absorbance in the presence and absence of UA, respectively) was used to measure the maximum value of the experimental conditions. The results showed that the optimal pH value in the system was determined to be 4.0 (Fig. S12a). The incubation temperature gradually increased in the range of 35–60 °C and reached a plateau at 50 °C (Fig. S12b). As shown in Figs. S12c–d, the incubation time and OPD concentration reached the platform at 18 min and 3 mM, respectively. The following experiments were conducted under the best conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of UA by dual-mode method
ce:section.ce:para: A dual-mode biosensor was constructed under optimal conditions to achieve sensitive detection of UA. According to the previously discussed mechanism, there is a good linear relationship between the absorbance of the system and the concentration of UA. Similarly, the ratio of proportional fluorescence I 560/I 470 also has a linear relationship with the increase of UA concentration. The absorbance at 450 nm was linearly related to the concentration of UA in the range of 0.4–200 μM (Fig. 5c). The linear equation was ΔA = 0.0048CUA+0.0143 (R2 = 0.994), and the LOD (S/N = 3) was 0.27 μM (Fig. 5d). As shown in Fig. 5e–f, I 560/I 470 increased linearly with the increase of UA concentration in the range of 0.2–200 μM. The linear equation was I 560/I 470 = 0.0136CUA+0.477 (R2 = 0.998), and the detection limit was 0.13 μM. Significant changes from blue fluorescence to yellow fluorescence were recorded by CIE (Commission Internationale de l ‘Eclairage) standard chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 5g). Table 1 shows the comparison of the analytical performance of the dual-mode biosensor constructed in this paper with those of other methods. The dual-mode method is comparable to or even better than previous reports in the linear range and LOD of UA, indicating that our method has better sensitivity and sensing range.

The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the dual-mode sensor were evaluated. In the actual detection of UA content in human blood, it is necessary to specifically recognize UA in human blood. Inorganic cations, inorganic anions, urea, amino acids, glucose, ascorbic acid (AA), cysteine (L-Cys), dopamine hydrochloride (DA), glutathione (GSH) and other common interfering substances in serum and urine were selected for experimental analysis. In this experiment, the concentration of UA was controlled at 40 μM, and other interfering substances such as ions, glucose, urea, were 2 mM. The concentrations of L-Cys, DA and GSH are all 20 μM. Although they are all reducing substances, they are reasonably low in human serum and urine (serum AA: 50–70 μM; L-Cys: about 15 μM; DA: 0.02–0.2 nM; GSH:5–10 μM. urine AA: 5.6–84 μM; L-Cys: 1–5 μM; DA: 0.1–2 μM; GSH: 0.1–1 μM) [49–53]. The concentration of AA in body serum is about 50–70 μM under normal conditions, but this value may vary due to individual dietary intake, health status, and detection methods. However, the concentration of ascorbic acid in serum may be affected by oxidation conditions, which will make its actual concentration low. As shown in Fig. 5h and 5i, the absorbance at 450 nm and the value of I 560/I 470 were not affected when the interfering substance coexisted with the UA to be detected. Experiments show that the dual-mode method has good selectivity and anti-interference ability for UA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of UA in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practicability and accuracy of the biosensor, it was used to analyze UA in human blood and urine. A total of 20 serum samples were detected and analyzed by dual-mode method. The analysis results were quite similar to the results of biochemical analyzer (Table 2 ). RSD is less than 4.2 %, showing good accuracy and precision. As shown in Table S3, the recovery of 2 human urine samples was conducted, and the sensing performance was verified again. The results showed that the recovery rates of fluorescence method and colorimetric method based on the nanozyme were 92.0–107.5 % and 106.5–108.5 %, respectively, and the RSD was less than 4.2 %. The two detection modes have good recovery rate and low relative error.

Principal component analysis (PCA) reduces the dimension of large data sets to the principal component that retains most of the original information, which is achieved by transforming potential related variables into a smaller set of variables (called principal components). As a typical dimension reduction technology, PCA is widely used in various fields because of its simplicity and effectiveness. The absorbance of an ultraviolet wavelength and the change of two fluorescence signal values were input as three-channel signals for pattern recognition. PCA was used to establish a model that could distinguish gout patients from healthy people. The composition of the calibration set is in the concentration range of 200–600 μM because it is generally desirable that the normal concentration range of UA in whole blood is 130–460 μM. According to the score plot (Fig. 6 a), the calibration set samples can be divided into different variety groups based on the clustering result, indicating that the established predictive model for UA is reliable and robust. It is proved that the theoretical conditions can be used to distinguish healthy people and gout patients by principal component analysis. Another set of samples consisting of 20 human serum samples was then used to predict and assess the risk by the calibration model. The results (Fig. 6b) showed that there was a certain intersection between healthy patients and gout patients, and they could not be completely separated, possibly because of the existence of potential group. Therefore, the population is divided into three categories: healthy population, potential population, and gout patients. According to Fig. 6c, it is appropriate to divide 20 samples into three clusters, which seemed related to the risk level of gout. The prediction set and PCA prove that the proposed sensor has good accuracy in the detection of UA in actual samples and can also distinguish and evaluate the gout risk.

In this paper, FeCuS nanoflowers were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method, and N-CDs were successfully loaded on FeCuS nanoflowers to generate FeCuS/N-CDs nanozymes. The obtained materials were characterized and confirmed by various instrumental techniques. Compared with natural enzymes, FeCuS/N-CDs nanozymes have the advantages of high stability, good catalytic activity, convenient preparation and low price. The Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and the maximum initial velocity (V max) of FeCuS/N-CDs are 1.57 mM and 28.6 × 10−8 M s−1, respectively. Based on its excellent catalytic effect, a sensing mechanism for accurate detection of UA by dual-mode method was proposed and used for the detection of UA in human serum. FeCuS/N-CDs catalyze H2O2 to generate •OH and O2 •-, oxidize OPD to DAP, and the color of the system changes from colorless to orange. At the same time, the fluorescence of FeCuS/N-CDs nanozyme at 470 nm was slowly quenched by DAP through IFE, while the characteristic fluorescence peak of DAP at 560 nm was gradually enhanced. Based on this, a fluorescence-colorimetric dual mode was constructed to detect UA in human body. The linear ranges were 0.2–200 μM and 0.4–200 μM, respectively, and the LODs were as low as 0.13 and 0.27 μM. PCA was used to successfully distinguish three groups of people with different uric acid levels.

Rou Cheng: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Chunyan Yan: Data curation. Zhengyue Xiao: Software. Xiaomin Tang: Resources. Peng Xu: Validation, Supervision. Ping Qiu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

The study was approved by the ethics committee of Jiangxi Medical College, and their guidelines were followed throughout this study. The serum samples involved in our research were from gout individuals. Informed consent was obtained from all human participants.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (21765015, 21808099) and the Science and the open project program of State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University, China (No. SKLF-KF-202416).

Jiang, Portable smartphone platform integrated with a nanoprobe-based fluorescent paper strip: visual monitoring of glutathione in human serum for health prognosis, ACS Sustainable Chemistry & Engineering, 8 (2020) 8175-8183.
